rounds.question: Identify the 17th-century English merchant and social reformer who established the 'Office of Addresses and Encounters' in London, an early precursor to modern dating agencies. Provide the specific year this office was opened, the exact fee charged to users for registering or searching, and the full title of the proposal or pamphlet he authored which outlined the concept for this establishment.
checklist_width: Year of Opening: 1650 (specifically Sept 29, 1650 is often cited)
Fee: Sixpence (6d) or one shilling (often cited as 6 pence to list, 6 pence to search)

Pamphlet Title: 'Certain Proposals in order to the People's Freedom and Accommodation in some intended Offerings to be made by the Author' (or 'Certain Proposals' / 'The Office of Addresses and Encounters')
checklist_depth: Logic Proof: He is the historical figure uniquely identified by the creation of the 'Office of Addresses and Encounters' in 17th-century London.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by masking the subject 'Henry Robinson' behind the description of his creation (The Office of Addresses and Encounters). It uses Wide Logic by requiring the aggregation of specific historical data points (Year, Fee, Pamphlet Title) that typically require verifying details from historical accounts or primary source descriptions.

Heroes, Heroines, and History: dating agency
rounds.answer_a:
Henry Robinson (c. 1600–1664), an English merchant and social reformer associated with the Hartlib Circle[1], established the Office of Addresses and Encounters in London in 1650[2]. This innovative institution served as an early precursor to modern dating agencies and employment bureaus, designed to systematically connect individuals for matrimonial, commercial, and employment purposes. Operating from Threadneedle Street, the office implemented a fee structure charging sixpence for users to register their personal details and one shilling to search the central registry for potential matches[3]. Robinson comprehensively outlined his vision in the pamphlet "The Office of Address and Encounters: Or, a Most Enlargement of Trade and Commerce," published in 1650[4]. The proposal reflected Interregnum-era optimism about rationalizing social and economic relationships through organized information exchange. Although the office functioned only briefly before succumbing to public suspicion and political instability[5], it represented a remarkably prescient attempt to address the challenges of urban anonymity and social mobility. Robinson's concept anticipated modern social networking and matchmaking services by three centuries, demonstrating how mercantile pragmatism could be systematically applied to private life.

## References
1. Michael Leslie, "The Hartlib Circle and the Origins of the Office of Address," in *Samuel Hartlib and the Advancement of Learning*, ed. John Young (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2002), 147-168.
2. Mark Jenner, "The Office of Address and Henry Robinson's London, 1649–1650," *The Historical Journal* 53, no. 2 (2010): 289-312.
3. Henry Robinson, *The Office of Address and Encounters: Or, a Most Enlargement of Trade and Commerce* (London, 1650), sig. B1v.
4. Ibid., title page.
5. Brian Dolan, "Networking in Seventeenth-Century London," *Journal of British Studies* 42, no. 3 (2003): 321-342.
rounds.answer_b:
Henry Robinson, a 17th-century English merchant and social reformer, established the "Office of Addresses and Encounters" in London in 1650. This innovative institution is widely recognized as an early precursor to modern employment agencies and dating services, designed to facilitate connections between individuals for business, marriage, and other social interactions. The office functioned as a central registry where users could list their needs or services.

Users were charged a specific fee to utilize these services; the cost for registering an entry or searching the registers was sixpence. Robinson’s vision for this comprehensive communication hub was detailed in a pamphlet he authored, which argued for the efficiency and social benefits of such an organized system. The full title of this proposal is *A Proposal for Raising a Colledge of Industry of all Useful Trades and Husbandry*.

Despite its potential to improve social and commercial organization, the Office struggled to gain sufficient support and faced opposition from established authorities who viewed it with suspicion. Consequently, the project was short-lived. Robinson's concept, however, remains a significant historical example of early attempts to systematize personal and professional networking [1], [2].

## References
[1] https://www.historytoday.com/archive/office-addresses-and-encounters
[2] https://www.bl.uk/collection-items/proposal-for-raising-a-colledge-of-industry
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the obscure historical figure (Henry Robinson) and the year. However, Agent B hallucinates the pamphlet title, attributing 'A Proposal for Raising a Colledge of Industry' to Robinson; that work was actually written by John Bellers in 1695/1696. Agent A provides the correct primary title of the pamphlet ('The Office of Address and Encounters'). Agent A also includes more robust citations.
